name: Amelia Poncia — The Spanish Republicans
description:
The year was 1931, in the midst of tensions within the Spaniard government and democratic movements from civilians, the Spanish Crown abdicates, and a republic was proclaimed for the first time since the short-lived republican experiment from 1873-1874.

Then, on July 17th, 1936, the Spaniard army forces in Spanish Morocco led by both Emilio Mola and Francisco Franco launch a coup and march their way into the Spanish mainland, sparking the start of the Spanish Civil War.

Among the soldiers that make up the Spanish Republican Army, is Amelia Poncia, an anthro female Iberian lynx who took up arms and now rallies behind the Spaniard tricolor after her family was taken hostage as a symbolic ransom by a group of Falangist sympathizers.

(Image Source: Me; used various images to create this character vis photoshop & doodle editing. | Inspired by the style used by both Droll3 & JetGranto for their WW2-era furry OCs on FurAffinity.)

⬇️ Lore Below: ⬇️

================

Amelia Poncia sits at the dinner table, the clatter of silverware a familiar soundtrack to her parents' heated discussions. The air in their Madrid apartment, heavy with the scent of garlic and paprika, is often thick with words like progreso, monarquía, and reforma. Her father, a history professor, gestures with a half-eaten bread roll, his voice rising with each point. "Spain," he declares, "is a patient in need of a cure. We cling to the old ways, the fiefdoms of the Church and the landed aristocracy, while the rest of Europe sprints ahead. We are stagnant." Her mother, a quiet but firm law clerk, nods in agreement, her gaze fixed on her husband. "Change is not merely desired," she says, her voice a low counterpoint, "it is a biological imperative for survival."

This is Amelia's life: a constant lesson in the socio-political ailments of her homeland. She absorbs their words, the theories of class struggle and the call for a new, modern Spain, with the same earnestness she applies to her studies. She learns not just history, but the why of it, the forces that keep Spain's boot stuck in the mud of the past.

Then, in 1931, the world shifts. The king abdicates, and the Second Spanish Republic is proclaimed. A wave of euphoria washes over the city. Church bells ring, people—human and anthro alike—spill into the streets, and the tricolor flag flies from every balcony. Amelia watches her parents, usually so composed, cheering with the crowd. For them, it is not just a political event; it is a personal victory, the first step on the path they have long advocated for. The future, they believe, is bright.

But the light of that hope begins to dim. The conversations at the dinner table grow darker. Her father speaks of the infighting within the Republican ranks, the deep schisms that threaten to tear their fragile coalition apart. Her mother details the assassinations and political violence that have begun to stain the news. "The traditionalists," she says, "are not going quietly. They see this as an insult to God and Crown." The murmurs of discontent from monarchists, the threats of military officers, violence between the left and right-winged groups, and the defiant strikes of the workers all paint a picture of a nation teetering on a precipice. It is not just progress versus tradition; it is chaos versus order, a fight where both sides refuse to compromise. The storm is coming, and it is no longer a theoretical debate.
